card.intuition: Maintain a 'tails' array where tails[i] is the smallest tail element of all increasing subsequences of length i+1. For each new number, binary search for its position in tails and replace or extend — giving O(n log n) overall.
card.analogy: Building stacks of cards in ascending order: place each new card on the leftmost stack whose top is >= the card, or start a new stack. The number of stacks is the LIS length. Greedy placement keeps each stack's top as small as possible.
card.time_complexity: O(n log n) with binary search; O(n²) naïve DP
when_to_use: Minimum number of increasing chains needed to cover a sequence (Dilworth's theorem: = LIS length)
Box stacking, chain of pairs problems reducible to LIS
gotchas: tails does NOT directly give the LIS itself — it gives the length. To reconstruct, track predecessors separately.
bisect_left finds the first position ≥ num; use bisect_right for non-strict (non-decreasing) sequences
The tails array is always sorted, enabling binary search

exercise.prompt: Implement O(n log n) LIS using binary search on the tails array.
hints: Replace tails[pos] with num — a smaller tail at position pos allows future numbers more room to extend the subsequence
exercise.test_code:
assert lis_length([10,9,2,5,3,7,101,18]) == 4   # [2,3,7,101] or [2,5,7,101]
assert lis_length([0,1,0,3,2,3]) == 4              # [0,1,2,3]
assert lis_length([7,7,7,7]) == 1                  # strictly increasing -> length 1
assert lis_length([1,2,3,4,5]) == 5
assert lis_length([5,4,3,2,1]) == 1
assert lis_length([1]) == 1
print('PASS')
